perturbation.original_text: A Confidential Information@ shall mean any and all information that a Party may reasonably expect to remain confidential and not shared with the general public at any time or that information that is protected by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall mean any information that a Party deems confidential, irrespective of its public availability or legal protection.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition aligns with standard confidentiality concepts, including reasonable expectation and legal protection. The modified definition allows a party to unilaterally declare any information confidential, regardless of its public status or existing legal protections, thus broadening the definition beyond legally accepted boundaries.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. The modified text's allowance for deeming publicly available information as 'confidential' directly contradicts this requirement, as information generally known cannot derive independent economic value from not being known.

perturbation.justification: The modified text allows a party to deem any information confidential regardless of public availability. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the UTSA definition of a trade secret because information generally known cannot derive independent economic value from not being known. The original text uses the term 'Confidential Information@' while the changed text uses the term 'Confidential Information', which has a statutory meaning and should not be changed. Because the changed text deviates from legally recognized definitions, it makes the contract unenforceable, fitting the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: It is expressly understood and agreed that (i) in the performance of Services under this Agreement, the Business Affiliate shall at all times act as an independent contractor with respect to UDC and not as an employee or agent of UDC and (ii) nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed to create a joint venture, partnership, association or other affiliation, or like relationship, between the Parties, it being specifically agreed that the relationship is and shall remain that of independent Parties to a contractual relationship.
perturbation.changed_text: It is expressly understood and agreed that (i) in the performance of Services under this Agreement, the Business Affiliate shall at all times act as an independent contractor with respect to UDC and not as an employee or agent of UDC; however, UDC retains the right to control the manner and means by which the Business Affiliate performs the services, including setting specific daily schedules and requiring detailed reports.
perturbation.explanation: While the text states the Business Affiliate is an independent contractor, the added clause gives UDC significant control over the affiliate's work. This level of control can lead to the worker being misclassified, and treated like an employee instead of an independent contractor.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the code defines 'employee', where employers have specific tax responsibilities. Retaining control over the 'manner and means' suggests an employer-employee relationship for tax purposes, regardless of the contractual language.

Definitions (a) Wages For purposes of this chapter, the term "wages" means all remuneration for employment, including the cash value of all remuneration (including benefits) paid in any medium other than cash; except that such term shall not include- (1) in the case of the taxes imposed by sections 3101(a) and 3111(a) that part of th
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the original intent of establishing an independent contractor relationship. The added clause grants UDC control over the 'manner and means' of the Business Affiliate's work. While snippet 2 is missing, Snippet 1 defines 'wages' in terms of 'remuneration for employment', highlighting the employer's responsibility. This contradicts the original text and the explicit statement defining the Business Affiliate as an independent contractor. The level of control given to UDC in the changed text suggests an employer-employee relationship, creating a contradiction because the contract now contains elements of both, thus creating a contradiction of misaligned terminology. This contradiction is significant because it affects tax and liability responsibilities.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby covenant and agree to indemnify and hold harmless each other, it's agents and representatives from and against any and all losses, costs, expenses, liabilities, claims, demands, judgments and its settlements of every nature that are actually incurred by a Party, including without limitation reasonable attorney's fees for pre-trial, trial, appellate, judgment, post-judgment, execution, and alternative dispute resolution, which arise out of any use or disclosure of Confidential Information not specifically permitted by this Agreement or arise out of the services rendered by Business Affiliate.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereby covenant and agree to indemnify and hold harmless each other, it's agents and representatives from and against any and all losses, costs, expenses, liabilities, claims, demands, judgments and its settlements of every nature that are allegedly incurred by a Party, including attorney's fees only if pre-approved in writing by the indemnifying party, which arise out of any use or disclosure of Confidential Information not specifically permitted by this Agreement or arise out of the services rendered by Business Affiliate.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'actually incurred' with 'allegedly incurred' and adds a requirement for pre-approval of attorney's fees. This creates uncertainty about what constitutes an indemnifiable loss, as 'allegedly' is vague, and the pre-approval clause can hinder a party's ability to defend itself.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute addresses indemnity agreements. While generally permitting them, modifications like 'allegedly incurred' and the pre-approval of attorney's fees can be challenged as attempts to limit the scope of reasonable and legitimately incurred losses. This could make the indemnity clause unenforceable, as the statute presumes a good-faith effort to provide indemnity for *actual* losses.

Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XLI STATUTE OF FRAUDS, FRAUDULENT TRANSFERS, AND GENERAL ASSIGNMENTS Chapter 725 UNENFORCEABLE CONTRACTS Entire Chapter SECTION 01 Promise to pay another’s debt, etc. F.S. 725.01 725.01 Promise to pay another’s debt, etc. — No action shall be brought whereby to charge any executor or administrator upon any special promise to answer or pay any debt or damages out of her or his own estate, or whereby to charge the defendant upon any special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person or to charge any person upon any agreement made upon consideration of marriage, or upon any contract for the sale of lands, tenements or hereditaments, or of any uncertain interest in or concerning them, or for any lease thereof for a period longer than 1 year, or upon any agreement that is not to be performed within the space of 1 year from the making thereof, or whereby to charge any health care provider upon any guarantee, warranty, or assurance as to the results of any medical, surgical, or diagnostic procedure performed by any physician licensed under chapter 458, osteopathic physician licensed under chapter 459, chiropractic physician licensed under chapter 460, podiatric physician licensed under chapter 461, or dentist licensed under chapter 466, unless the agreement or promise upon which such action shall be brought, or some note or memorandum thereof shall be in writing and signed by the party to be charged therewith or by some other person by her or him thereunto lawfully authorized. History. — s. 10, Nov. 15, 1828; RS 1995; GS 2517; RGS 3872; CGL 5779; s. 10, ch. 75-9; s. 933, ch. 97-102; s. 60, ch. 97-264; ss. 227, 294, ch. 98-166.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces 'allegedly incurred' and requires pre-approval for attorney's fees, which contradicts the original intent of indemnification for actual losses. While the provided Florida Statute 725.01 addresses promises to pay another's debt, it doesn't directly cover indemnity agreements. However, the Law Explanation suggests these modifications could limit the scope of legitimately incurred losses, potentially making the indemnity clause unenforceable due to undermining the good-faith effort to indemnify for *actual* losses.
